Documentation · reads bottom-up from zero

Job Agent — The Complete Guide

Everything that happens inside your project, explained from scratch: what every file does, how the AI is used (with the actual instructions it's given), what the important code means line by line, and why things were built the way they were.

Contents
  1. What this project is
  2. The basics, from zero
  3. A map of the whole system
  4. Where your data lives
  5. The app you see (the front end)
  6. The server (the back end)
  7. Searching: 10 boards + real companies
  8. Ranking: how jobs get a match score
  9. Writing documents: the AI writer, critic & fixer
  10. Hebrew & two-language support
  11. The form-filler robot
  12. Tools & the agent loop
  13. The AI plumbing: one gate, retries & JSON
  14. Memory & autopilot
  15. Desktop vs. website (Vercel)
  16. How we know it works
  17. Changing things yourself
  18. Glossary

1 · What this project is

Applying for a job by hand means: finding postings on five different sites, re-typing your details into yet another form, rewriting your resume so it matches this posting, writing yet another cover letter, and answering the same twenty questions. An hour per application, most of it copy-paste work that requires no thinking.

Job Agent is a program that does that repetitive part for you:

Profileyou describe yourself once
→
Search10 job sites + company career pages, at once
→
Rankevery job scored against you
→
Writetailored resume + cover letter, fact-checked
→
You submitthe only manual step

It can even open the employer's real application form in your browser and fill it in — then it stops, highlights anything it couldn't answer, and waits for you to press Submit. By design, the software is never allowed to send an application on its own.

The main screen: one search box, results ranked best-first, each with a match score, a plain-language reason, and a Skip button.

2 · The basics, from zero

Five ideas are enough to understand everything below. If you know these, skip ahead.

A program and Python

A program is a list of instructions a computer follows. Ours are written in Python, a programming language known for reading almost like English. All the "brain" files of this project (in the job_agent/ folder) are Python files — their names end in .py.

A server and a browser

The app is split in two halves that talk to each other:

  • The server (also called the back end) is the Python program running on your computer. It does the actual work: searching, scoring, writing documents, saving files.
  • The page in your browser (the front end) is what you see and click. It's built from three files: index.html (the content), style.css (the appearance) and app.js (the behavior — written in JavaScript, the language browsers speak).

An API and JSON

The two halves talk over an API — an agreed set of web addresses where the page can ask the server for things. For example, when you click Find jobs, the page sends a request to the address /api/search and the server replies with the results. The replies are formatted as JSON — a simple text format for structured data that both halves (and the AI) understand. It looks like this:

{ "title": "Bookkeeper", "company": "Acme", "remote": true }

An LLM (the "AI")

An LLM (large language model) is a program trained on enormous amounts of text that can read and write language. You give it written instructions (a prompt) plus your material, and it writes back. This project uses a free LLM service called Groq running a model named llama-3.3-70b. Important mental model: the LLM is not "the program" — it's more like a very fast intern the program consults at five specific moments, always with strict instructions, and always with its work checked.

The terminal

A text window where you type commands to the computer directly. You almost never need it — double-clicking Job Agent.command does everything — but the project also offers a full terminal interface for power users (Part 12).

3 · A map of the whole system

Here is the entire project, folder by folder. Bold names are the ones worth remembering.

agent/ ├── Job Agent.command ← double-click this: sets up + starts everything ├── app.py tiny doorway used when hosted on Vercel ├── run.py tiny doorway for the terminal version ├── requirements.txt the list of building blocks to install (Flask, Groq, …) ├── job_agent/ THE PROGRAM — every brain lives here │ ├── web.py the server: all /api/… addresses, autopilot │ ├── scraper.py searches the 10 job boards + company career pages │ ├── company_directory.py which companies to check per country │ ├── matching.py turns a job + your profile into a 0–100 score │ ├── generate.py writes resume + cover letter; AI writer, critic, fixer │ ├── autofill.py fills real application forms in your Chrome │ ├── profile_store.py the shape of "you": every profile field + Q&A │ ├── tracker.py remembers every job you've seen and its status │ ├── pdfgen.py turns documents into PDF files using Chrome │ ├── llm.py the one gateway to the AI (pacing, retries, repairs) — Part 13 │ ├── tools.py + agent.py the "agent" machinery — Part 12 │ ├── cli.py the terminal commands │ ├── config.py / util.py settings and small helpers │ └── static/ THE PAGE — what your browser shows │ ├── index.html every screen's content (hidden/shown as needed) │ ├── app.js every click's behavior │ ├── catalogs.js the big option lists behind all the dropdowns │ └── style.css colors, spacing, layout ├── presentation/ your class slides + PowerPoint ├── tests/e2e_test.py the automated "pretend user" test (Part 16) └── docs/ this guide

And how the pieces stack at runtime:

Web pageindex.html + app.js in your browser
Terminalpython -m job_agent …
Plain English"find me remote python jobs"
↓ all three call the same engine ↓
web.py — the server & tool registryevery capability defined once; callable by code or by the AI
↓
scraperfind jobs
matchingscore them
generatewrite documents
autofillfill real forms
trackerremember
↓ talks to the outside world ↓
Job board APIsRemotive, RemoteOK, …
Career-page APIsGreenhouse · Lever · Ashby · SmartRecruiters
Groqthe LLM
Your ChromePDFs + form filling

4 · Where your data lives

Everything about you is stored in ordinary files on your own computer, in a hidden folder called .job_agent inside your home folder. Nothing is kept on anyone's server.

FileWhat's inside
profile.jsonYou: name, contact details, experience, education, skills, preferences, saved answers. Written by the Profile screen; read by everything else.
jobs_cache.jsonThe raw jobs found by your last search.
ranked.jsonThe same jobs with their match scores, best first.
seen.jsonThe tracker: every job you've ever seen and its status (new / skipped / prepared / submitted / interview / rejected).
settings.jsonYour automation knobs and optional job-source keys.
applications/One folder per prepared application: resume.pdf, resume.txt, cover_letter.pdf, application_form.md (all your copy-paste answers), job.md (the posting), answers.json.
companies_cache.jsonCompanies the AI discovered for your country, after verification (Part 7).
.envYour Groq AI key when saved from the app.

A taste of what profile.json actually looks like inside (shortened):

{
  "full_name": "דוד זף",
  "contact": { "email": "…", "country": "Israel" },
  "skills": ["Accounting", "Microsoft Excel", …],
  "experience": [ { "title": "…", "company": "…", "achievements": […] } ],
  "preferences": { "desired_titles": ["Data Entry Clerk", "Accountant"], "remote_ok": true }
}
In plain words: the profile is the single source of truth. The AI is never allowed to say anything about you that isn't in this file — that rule is enforced twice (Part 9).

5 · The app you see (the front end)

index.html contains all the screens at once — Welcome, Profile, Find jobs, Applications, and the application detail view. They're stacked invisibly; JavaScript shows exactly one at a time. That's why the file is full of sections like:

<section id="view-find" class="view" hidden> … </section>

and why app.js has a tiny function that flips which one is visible:

function switchView(name) {
  $$('.view').forEach(v => { v.hidden = true; });   // hide every screen ①
  $('#view-' + name).hidden = false;                // show the one asked for ②
  …
}
  • 1$$('.view') means "find every element whose class is view" — all the screens — and the arrow code runs once per screen, hiding it.
  • 2Then the single requested screen (for example view-find) is un-hidden.

The Profile screen. Nearly every field is a dropdown or a click-to-add "chip" — the option lists (200 skills, 140 job titles, 129 countries…) live in catalogs.js.

How the page talks to the server

Every click funnels through one little helper in app.js:

async function api(path, opts = {}) {
  const headers = {};
  const key = localStorage.getItem('ja_key');        // ①
  if (key) headers['X-Groq-Key'] = key…;
  const res = await fetch('/api/' + path, init);      // ②
  let data = await res.json();                        // ③
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error(data.error || 'Something went wrong…');
  return data;                                        // ④
}
  • 1On the hosted (website) version, your AI key is stored in the browser's own pocket, called localStorage, and rides along with each request. The server never keeps it.
  • 2fetch is the browser's built-in way of calling a web address — here, the server's API.
  • 3The reply text is decoded from JSON into data the code can use.
  • 4If the server said something went wrong, the error message is surfaced to you as a toast (the little black popup); otherwise the data is handed to whoever asked.

The page keeps a handful of variables as its short-term memory while open — the main ones:

VariableHolds
PYour whole profile (mirrors profile.json). Editing the form edits P; Save sends it to the server (or to localStorage when hosted).
RESULTSThe current ranked search results shown as cards.
SEEN / APPS_LSHosted mode only: your job history and prepared applications, kept in the browser.
STATUSWhat the server reported on load: is AI on, how many sources, am I hosted, the build stamp (Part 15).

6 · The server (the back end)

web.py is the switchboard. It's built with Flask, a small Python library for making web servers: you write ordinary functions and label each with the web address that should trigger it. All addresses the page uses:

AddressWhat it does
GET /Serves the page itself (with a version stamp — Part 15).
GET /api/status"Who am I talking to?" — profile exists? AI on? tracker counts, settings, build stamp.
GET/POST /api/profileRead / save your profile.
POST /api/searchRun a search across all sources, rank it, remember new jobs.
POST /api/rerankAsk the AI to re-score the top matches more carefully.
POST /api/applyPrepare one application (documents + PDFs + fact-check).
GET /api/applications, GET /api/application/<name>List / open prepared applications.
POST /api/autofillFill the employer's real form in your Chrome (desktop only).
POST /api/answerAnswer any application question from your profile.
POST /api/trackChange a job's status (skipped, submitted…).
POST /api/github/importTurn your public GitHub projects into resume entries.
GET/POST /api/settings, POST /api/settings/keyAutomation knobs / save & live-test the AI key.
GET /api/progressThe one-line "what is the AI doing right now" note the progress window polls.

Here's the search endpoint, slightly shortened — a typical example of how the server glues the engine together:

@app.post("/api/search")                              // ①
def search():
    data = request.get_json(…) or {}
    c = Context(profile=_profile_from(data), llm=None)  // ②
    keywords = […] or ps.default_keywords(c.profile)     // ③
    jobs = scraper.search_jobs(keywords, …,
        country=c.profile.contact.country,               // ④
        config=_source_config(data), llm=_req_llm())
    ranked = matching.rank_jobs(c.profile, jobs)          // ⑤
    c.save_ranked(ranked)
    new_count = tracker.record_seen([e["job"] for e in ranked])  // ⑥
    return jsonify({"count": len(ranked), "new": new_count, "results": …})
  • 1The label: "when the page POSTs to /api/search, run this function."
  • 2Load your profile — from disk normally, or from the request itself on the hosted version (the browser sends it along, since the hosted server keeps nothing).
  • 3If you typed nothing, fall back to the job titles from your profile.
  • 4The actual search (Part 7) — note your country goes in, which activates the company-careers source.
  • 5Score and sort everything against your profile (Part 8).
  • 6The tracker notes which of these jobs you've never seen before, so the page can say "8 new since last time."

The autopilot thread

When the desktop app is open, a background loop wakes every few minutes and checks whether it's time for an automatic search (default: every 6 hours). If you set a score threshold in Settings, it also prepares applications by itself for new jobs above that score — capped per run so it can't run away with your AI quota. Results wait for you under Applications.

What actually broke here once: your very first "the home page is blank" bug wasn't in this code at all — a forgotten python -m http.server from June 23 was squatting the app's address (port 8765), so the browser was talking to the wrong program entirely. The server now checks its port at startup: it replaces a stale old Job Agent, steps to the next free port if a stranger owns it, and always opens the browser at the address it really bound.

7 · Searching: 10 boards + real companies

scraper.py knows how to talk to ten public job sites (Remotive, RemoteOK, Arbeitnow, Jobicy, Himalayas, WeWorkRemotely, Hacker News "Who is hiring", The Muse, Working Nomads — plus optional Jooble/Adzuna aggregators if you add free keys in Settings). Every site formats its data differently, so each has a small "fetcher" that translates its answers into one standard shape, a Job:

@dataclass
class Job:
    id: str          # a fingerprint, so the same job is never shown twice
    title: str
    company: str
    location: str
    remote: bool
    url: str         # where to actually apply
    description: str # the posting text, cleaned of HTML
    …

Each fetcher is wrapped in a safety net — if one site is down or changes its format, that site returns nothing and the other nine still work:

def fetch_remotive(sess, keywords, limit, opts=None):
    try:                                          # ① try…
        data = _get_json(sess, "https://remotive.com/api/remote-jobs", …)
        return [Job(…) for j in data["jobs"]]   # ② translate each result
    except Exception as e:                        # ③ …but never crash
        print(f"  [warn] remotive source failed: {e}")
        return []
  • 1try means "attempt this, and if anything goes wrong, jump to the except part instead of crashing."
  • 2A compact loop: for every job the site returned, build our standard Job from its fields.
  • 3On any failure: print a quiet warning, contribute zero jobs, move on.

Why the code does its own filtering

We discovered early that boards' own keyword search is unreliable — searching "python" on one site returned sales jobs, because postings carry spammy tag lists. So the aggregator re-checks every job itself and sorts matches into tiers:

Tier 3keyword in the job title — the only trustworthy signal
Tier 2keyword in company / tags
Tier 1keyword only somewhere in the description
Tier 0no match — dropped

Title matches fill the result list first; weaker matches only pad out what's left. Multi-word phrases match by words, so "Customer Support Specialist" also matches "Specialist, Customer Support".

Company career pages — automatic for your country

Most tech companies run their hiring on one of four systems (Greenhouse, Lever, Ashby, SmartRecruiters), and all four offer public addresses where anyone may read a company's openings. company_directory.py ships a list of 109 companies across 9 countries whose addresses we verified live — for Israel: NICE, Via, Cato Networks, Gong, Similarweb, Taboola, JFrog, Fireblocks, AppsFlyer and twenty more. Because your profile says country: Israel, every search quietly also asks all 29 Israeli boards — in parallel, about 2.5 seconds for ~1,100 live openings.

For a country not on the list, the AI is asked to suggest employers — and here's the important part — every suggestion is checked against the real career-page addresses before use, and only verified ones are kept (cached for 30 days). The AI can propose; reality decides. When we tested with Spain, it found Cabify, Typeform and Wallapop — 83 real openings.

Fairness rule: company-page jobs rank first (they're the most targeted), but they're capped at half the result list and rotated one-per-company — otherwise NICE's 334 openings would bury everything else.

8 · Ranking: how jobs get a match score

Scoring happens in two passes. The first costs nothing and runs on every job — plain arithmetic in matching.py:

score = 100 * ( 0.40 * skill_score      # your skills found in the posting
              + 0.27 * title_score      # posting title vs. titles you want
              + 0.18 * location_score   # remote/location fit
              + 0.15 * seniority_score )# junior/senior level fit

So a job where many of your skills appear, whose title matches what you're looking for, which you could actually attend, at your level, scores high. The card also explains itself — "1 matching skill: excel; title matches your target roles; gaps: git, go" — that text is generated from the same calculation.

The second pass is optional (the ✨ button): the AI reads the top ten postings against your full profile, like a recruiter would, and returns for each a score with a verdict and rationale. Its instructions pin it down:

"You are a technical recruiter… rate how good a fit the candidate is
 for THIS role. Be realistic and concise. Do not assume skills the
 candidate did not list. Return JSON with keys: score (0-100),
 verdict, rationale, strengths, gaps."

The final ranking blends both: half arithmetic, half judgment. Why only the top ten? The free AI service allows a limited number of calls per minute — the arithmetic pre-filter spends the AI budget only where it matters.

9 · Writing documents: the AI writer, critic & fixer

This is the heart of the project, in generate.py. When you click Prepare, three AI roles run in sequence — the multi-agent part of the system:

1 · Tailor AIwrites a draft resume summary, bullet points and cover letter for this specific job
→
2 · Reviewer AIhunts for claims not backed by your profile, wrong company, wrong language
→
3 · Repairer AIrewrites only the flagged problems
→
4 · Renderer codeassembles the actual documents + PDFs

The writer's leash

The Tailor's instructions (its system prompt) open with hard rules — this is the exact text the AI receives, shortened:

"You tailor a candidate's resume and write a cover letter for one
 specific job.
 CRITICAL RULES:
 - Use ONLY facts present in the candidate profile. Never invent
   employers, job titles, dates, degrees, certifications, metrics,
   or skills the candidate does not have.
 - LANGUAGE: write the summary, bullets, and cover letter in the
   language of the JOB POSTING (a Hebrew posting gets Hebrew
   documents) …
 Return a JSON object with keys: summary, ordered_skills,
 experience_bullets, cover_letter, keywords_used."

The cage of indexes — why it can't invent a job

Rules alone aren't enough — models sometimes ignore them. So the prompt adds a structural trap: your work history is sent as a numbered list, and the AI must return its bullet points keyed by those numbers. A job that isn't in your profile has no number — there is literally nowhere to hang invented bullets:

ROLES (index in brackets):
[0] Bookkeeper at Acme (2021–Present); tech: Excel…
[1] Data Entry Clerk at Initech (2019–2021); …

← what the AI must send back:
"experience_bullets": {"0": […], "1": […]}

One more dial worth knowing: temperature — how adventurous the AI is allowed to be, from 0 (same answer every time) upward. Each role gets its own setting: the Tailor writes at 0.35 (a little freedom of phrasing), the recruiter-Ranker judges at 0.2, and the Reviewer fact-checks at 0.1 — nearly deterministic. Creativity only where it's safe.

The critic that keeps it honest

LLMs sometimes make things up — confidently. Instead of hoping, the project assumes it will happen and checks. The Reviewer gets your profile labeled "the only source of truth" plus the draft, and must return a list of concrete problems, each typed:

FlagMeaning
UNSUPPORTEDA claim, metric, skill or degree that isn't in your profile.
WRONG_COMPANYThe letter addresses a different company than the posting.
PLACEHOLDERLeftover template text like "[Company]".
WRONG_LANGUAGEDraft not in the posting's language.
Proof it works: in testing we deliberately fed it a corrupted draft claiming a PhD in Physics, "10 years of experience", a Nobel Prize in databases, and a letter addressed to the wrong company. The Reviewer caught all five problems, the Repairer removed every one, and the re-check came back clean. It also catches subtle real cases — like the writer padding "strong communication skills" onto a profile that never claimed them.

If an AI step fails, the packet still ships

Every AI call in this chain can fail — the free service can be busy, an answer can arrive malformed. Each failure has a planned, harmless landing:

If this fails……this happens instead
TailorYou get the untailored (but complete and truthful) resume straight from your profile.
ReviewerThe draft is kept as written — a broken critic must never block the packet.
RepairerThe original draft is kept, and the reviewer's findings are saved into answers.json so nothing is hidden.

That's why "Prepare" never crashes: the AI steps are upgrades stacked on a working non-AI baseline — not links in a chain that snaps.

Code writes the layout, AI writes only words

The final documents are assembled by ordinary code from a fixed, single-column template (render_resume / render_resume_html): standard headings, no tables, no graphics. That's deliberate — employers run resumes through automatic parsers ("ATS" systems), and plain layouts parse reliably. The AI never controls layout; it only supplies sentences. PDFs are made by pdfgen.py, which quietly uses the Chrome already on your computer as a print engine — no extra software.

A finished application: status tracker, auto-fill button, every form field with a Copy button, tailored cover letter and resume with PDF downloads, and the answer-anything box.

What actually broke here once: PDF creation froze whenever the user's regular Chrome was already open — the old command style waited forever. The fix: run Chrome's new headless mode with a throwaway profile and watch for the output file instead of waiting for the program to exit.

10 · Hebrew & two-language support

Your profile is in Hebrew; postings come in Hebrew and English. Two independent problems had to be solved:

Which language should the documents be in?

Rule: the posting decides, not the profile. A tiny detector counts Hebrew letters in the posting:

def detect_lang(text):
    hebrew = len(re.findall(r"[א-ת]", text))     # count Hebrew letters
    latin  = len(re.findall(r"[A-Za-z]", text))  # count English letters
    return "he" if hebrew ≥ 25% of latin else "en"

…and the result is written into the AI's instructions explicitly ("OUTPUT LANGUAGE: Hebrew — translate profile facts as needed but keep company and technology names unchanged"). Section headings follow along: Professional Summary becomes תקציר מקצועי, and the sign-off Sincerely, becomes בברכה,.

Making mixed text display correctly

Hebrew reads right-to-left, but resumes mix in English tech names, numbers and dates. Browsers get list bullets and side-by-side layouts wrong unless each block declares its direction, so the renderer detects direction per line and stamps it on: bullet dots sit on the right for Hebrew lines, role titles sit right with dates left, and "ניהלתי תקציב של 250,000 ש״ח" keeps its numbers and commas in the right places.

What actually broke here once: Hebrew abbreviations like ש"ח contain a quote character — which is also JSON's quote character — so the AI's answers arrived as invalid JSON and the whole tailoring step failed. Two-layer fix: the AI is told to use the proper Hebrew gershayim mark (ש״ח), and if it forgets, llm.py repairs quotes squeezed between Hebrew letters before reading the JSON.

11 · The form-filler robot

The most magical feature, in autofill.py. When a posting runs on Greenhouse or Lever (very common), one click opens a real Chrome window — your Chrome — on the employer's actual application form, filled in:

Detectwhich form system the posting uses; aggregator links are traced to the real form
→
Scana small script lists every field on the page with its label
→
Fillcode answers the standard fields; the AI answers open questions; resume PDF is uploaded
→
STOPunanswered fields glow orange; you review and press Submit

Filling is deliberately boring where it can be. Standard fields are matched by their labels with plain code — a taste of _basic_answer:

if has("first name", "given name"): return first
if has("e-mail", "email"):           return c.email
if has("linkedin"):                  return profile.links.linkedin
if has("notice period", "start date"): return p.availability …

Yes/no and identity questions (visa sponsorship, veteran status…) are answered from your saved preferences, with "prefer not to say" chosen where you left something blank. Only genuinely open questions ("Why do you want to work here?") go to the AI — with a budget cap of 8 AI calls per form. Consent checkboxes are never ticked automatically.

A real company's form (GitLab's) after auto-fill: name, email, phone, resume attached — and the green banner reminding you that the last click is yours.

Why it can't do every site: portals like LinkedIn, Workday or iCIMS require logging in and actively block robots; automating them breaks constantly and violates their rules. Greenhouse/Lever forms are public pages — no login, no tricks — which is why the feature targets them and honestly says so otherwise.

Under the robot's hood

One thread owns the browser. Playwright (the browser-driving library) only accepts orders from the thread that started it — so every fill request is placed in a queue, and a single worker thread executes them one at a time. The browser itself is a persistent profile stored in ~/.job_agent/browser/, which is why logins and cookies survive between runs.

Aggregator links are traced to the real form. A posting found on a job board usually links to the board's own page, not the employer's form. The filler downloads that page and searches its text for the real address with two patterns:

boards.greenhouse.io/…     # also job-boards.… and the eu. variants
jobs.lever.co/…

The page is scanned by a tiny script. Before filling, a JavaScript snippet runs inside the page, tags every input with a serial number, and reports back each field's label, type, choices, and whether it's required — that list is the robot's map of the form.

One bad field never stops the rest. Every field is filled inside its own safety net; anything that goes wrong just lands that field on the "you review this" list. And the code that would click Submit simply does not exist — that is the guarantee.

12 · Tools & the agent loop

Beyond the buttons, the project has a genuine "AI agent" mode: type python -m job_agent agent "find remote python jobs, show top 3" in a terminal and the AI itself decides which capabilities to use. This works through tools.

What a tool is

A tool is a normal Python function plus a JSON description the AI can read (tools.py):

@dataclass
class Tool:
    name: str            # "search_jobs"
    description: str     # what it does, in words the AI reads
    parameters: dict     # what arguments it accepts (a JSON schema)
    func: Callable       # the real Python function to run

Six exist: search_jobs, rank_jobs, list_ranked, generate_application, answer_application_question, get_profile_summary. One registry holds them, and — the design move of the whole project — two different callers use the same registry: the fixed pipeline (buttons, CLI) calls tools directly in a set order, and the agent mode hands their descriptions to the AI and lets it choose.

The loop

agent.py is barely 80 lines. Its heart:

for _ in range(max_steps):                          # ① at most 8 rounds
    msg = llm.chat(messages, tools=schemas)          # ② AI sees tools, replies
    if not msg.tool_calls:                           # ③ plain text? we're done
        return msg.content
    for tc in msg.tool_calls:                        # ④ run what it asked for
        result = registry.dispatch(tc.name, tc.arguments)
        messages.append({"role": "tool", "content": result})  # ⑤
  • 1A hard ceiling so the AI can't loop forever.
  • 2The AI receives the conversation so far plus the six tool descriptions, and answers either with text or with "please run tool X with arguments Y".
  • 3If it answered in plain text, that's the final answer.
  • 4Otherwise we actually run the requested function — the AI never executes anything itself; it only asks.
  • 5The function's result is added to the conversation, and the loop repeats — now the AI knows what happened and decides its next step.

The agent's "memory" is just a growing list

The AI itself remembers nothing between calls — it is stateless. What creates the feeling of an assistant working through a task is the messages list: every request, decision and tool result is appended to it, and on each round the AI re-reads the whole list and picks the next move. Here is that list at the end of a run:

[system]     You are a job-application assistant…
[user]       "find remote python jobs, show top 3"
[assistant]  → search_jobs({"keywords": ["python"]})
[tool]       {"found": 14, "sample": […]}
[assistant]  → rank_jobs({"top": 3})
[tool]       {"ranked": 14, "top": […]}
[assistant]  "Your top 3 matches are: …"   ← plain text — loop over

What "run the tool" actually does

The AI never executes code. It only writes text: a tool's name plus arguments as JSON. dispatch() turns that text into action — parse the arguments, look up the tool, call the real Python function, return its result as JSON. And crucially, every possible failure goes back to the AI as a message instead of crashing the program:

{"error": "unknown tool 'serch_jobs'"}          # it typo'd the name
{"error": "arguments were not valid JSON"}      # malformed arguments
{"error": "bad arguments for search_jobs: …"}   # wrong parameter names
{"error": "ConnectionError: …"}                 # the tool itself failed

The error lands in the conversation like any other result; the AI reads it and fixes its next attempt. Errors become feedback.

Why tool replies are tiny

Look at what search_jobs reports back: a count, the cache file's location, and eight sample titles — roughly 80 tokens. Not the fourteen full postings, which would be ~15,000 tokens. The full data is written to jobs_cache.json, and the next tool reads it from there. The conversation carries references; the disk carries data. That keeps every round fast, keeps the AI focused, and keeps the whole loop inside the free tier's limits.

A real recorded run:

$ agent "find remote python jobs, show top 3, don't apply"
→ tool: search_jobs({"keywords":["python"],"remote":true})   {"found": 14}
→ tool: rank_jobs({"top": 3})                                {"ranked": 14}
→ tool: list_ranked({"top": 3})
"Your top 3 matches are: 1. Senior Full-stack Engineer at Truelogic…"
Design choice worth knowing: the everyday pipeline is not run by this loop — buttons call tools in a fixed order written in code. An AI orchestrator would add cost, delay, and new ways to fail for zero benefit when the right order is already known. The AI gets decision power only where judgment is genuinely needed.

13 · The AI plumbing: one gate, retries & JSON

Five different moments consult the AI — ranking, tailoring, reviewing, repairing, answering — but none of them talk to Groq directly. Every single call funnels through one file, llm.py, which absorbs the three ugly realities of using a free AI service:

Any AI momentranker, tailor, reviewer, repairer, answerer
→
Throttleone shared clock; every call waits its ≥2-second turn
→
Groqllama-3.3-70b answers
→
If refusedwait and retry — up to 5 times, pauses growing 2s→30s

Reality 1: rate limits. The free tier allows only so many requests (and so many words) per minute. So there's a throttle: a single clock, shared by every thread in the program, hands out time slots at least two seconds apart. Even when two applications are being prepared at the same time, their AI calls politely take turns.

Reality 2: refusals. Sometimes the service answers "too busy" (error 429) or hiccups (a 5xx error). The gate waits and retries with growing pauses — 2, 4, 8… capped at 30 seconds — and when the service says exactly how long to wait (a Retry-After header), that wish is honored. Real mistakes, like a badly formed request, are not retried; they surface immediately so they can be fixed.

Reality 3: JSON. Almost every AI answer in this project must be machine-readable JSON, but language models are talkative. Four rungs force the issue:

1 · Askevery system prompt ends: "Respond with a single valid JSON object and nothing else."
2 · EnforceGroq's "JSON mode" makes the service itself reject non-JSON output
3 · RetryJSON mode chokes on Hebrew quote marks (ש"ח) — on that specific failure, retry once without it
4 · Repairfind the {…} inside the reply, escape quotes squeezed between Hebrew letters, parse again

Only if all four rungs fail does the calling step fall back to its non-AI baseline (Part 9). This one file is the reason the rest of the project gets to treat a flaky, rate-limited AI service as a boring, dependable component.

14 · Memory & autopilot

tracker.py is forty lines that make the whole thing livable: a dictionary from job fingerprint to status, saved as seen.json.

newnever seen before
→
skippedyou said no — never resurfaces
prepareddocuments ready
→
submittedyou sent it
→
interview / rejectedthe outcome

Every search stamps its results against this memory — that's how the page can show "Show: new jobs" and honestly greet you with "no new jobs since last time." Combined with the background search (Part 6), the daily experience becomes: open the app → a short list of only-new, already-ranked jobs → sometimes with applications already prepared and fact-checked, waiting.

15 · Desktop vs. website (Vercel)

The same code runs two ways, chosen automatically by one flag (IS_HOSTED in config.py, set when Vercel is detected):

Desktop (Job Agent.command)Website (agent-five-ecru.vercel.app)
Your datafiles in ~/.job_agentyour own browser's storage (localStorage) — the server keeps nothing
AI keysaved to a local filesaved in your browser, sent along with each request
Form auto-fill✔ (drives your Chrome)✘ — a website can't operate your browser; explained in-app
Background autopilot✔ while the app is open✘ — the hosted server only exists during a request
PDF files✔ made with your ChromePrint / save-as-PDF button instead

Why the difference: Vercel runs "serverless" — your request wakes a copy of the program, it answers, and it evaporates. No disk that persists, no program that stays alive. So the hosted version was redesigned to be stateless: the browser carries the profile with each request and keeps everything personal. Privacy-wise this is the best arrangement: your data never sits on a server at all.

What actually broke here (your blank Vercel page): browsers keep copies of pages to load faster. Yours kept an old copy of the page while fetching new behavior code; the mismatch crashed startup before any screen was shown. Three-layer fix now in place: pages are served with "always re-check" caching instructions; startup tolerates missing pieces instead of dying; and every page carries a build stamp — on load it compares itself with the server and, if outdated, reloads itself once through a cache-proof address. Stale pages heal themselves.

16 · How we know it works

tests/e2e_test.py is a robot user built on Playwright (a tool that drives a real browser). It starts a fresh server with an empty home folder and lives a complete user journey — first visit, welcome screen, filling the profile through the real form, saving, reloading, searching live job boards, skipping a job, preparing an application with the full AI writer-critic-fixer chain, opening it, downloading, changing status, asking the answer-box a question, opening settings — asserting 21 checks along the way, and once more in a simulated Vercel environment (42 total). It also listens for any JavaScript error on any page: one uncaught error fails the run.

Run it yourself:

.venv/bin/python tests/e2e_test.py local
.venv/bin/python tests/e2e_test.py hosted

17 · Changing things yourself

You want to…Where to go
Add a job boardscraper.py: copy any fetch_… function, adapt it to the new site's format, add it to the SOURCES dict at the bottom. It's automatically searched and de-duplicated.
Add companies for a countrycompany_directory.py: add (slug, system, "Name") entries to BUILTIN — or just set your profile's country and let discovery find them.
Change how scoring weighs thingsmatching.py, the 0.40/0.27/0.18/0.15 line.
Change what the AI is toldgenerate.py: _TAILOR_SYSTEM (writer), _REVIEW_SYSTEM (critic); profile_store.py: _ANSWER_SYSTEM.
Add a dropdown optionstatic/catalogs.js — pure lists, one line per option.
Add a tool the agent can usetools.py, inside build_registry: write a function + register it with a description. Both the buttons and the AI can use it instantly.
Change automation defaultsconfig.py, APP_SETTINGS_DEFAULTS.

Useful environment switches (set in .env): GROQ_API_KEY (the AI key), JOB_AGENT_MODEL (which model), JOB_AGENT_HOME (where data lives), JOB_AGENT_MIN_INTERVAL (pause between AI calls).

18 · Glossary

APIAn agreed set of web addresses programs use to talk to each other.
ATSApplicant Tracking System — the software employers use to receive applications (Greenhouse, Lever…). Also why resumes must stay plainly formatted.
Back end / front endThe server doing the work / the page you see and click.
CacheA saved copy kept for speed; stale caches caused our blank-page saga.
DataclassPython shorthand for "a record with named fields" — used for Job, Profile, Tool.
EndpointOne API address, like /api/search.
FlaskThe small Python library our server is built with.
JSONThe universal text format for structured data.
LLMLarge language model — the AI that reads and writes text (here: llama-3.3-70b via Groq).
localStorageA browser's private pocket for a site's data — where the hosted version keeps your profile.
PlaywrightA tool that lets a program drive a real browser — powers form-filling and the tests.
PromptThe written instructions an LLM receives.
Rate limitHow many requests per minute a service accepts — the free AI tier's main constraint, absorbed by the gate in Part 13.
ServerlessHosting where the program exists only while answering a request (how Vercel runs it).
StatelessKeeping no memory between calls. The LLM is stateless (the message list is the agent's only memory); so is the hosted server.
TemperatureAn LLM dial from predictable (0) to adventurous. The writer runs at 0.35, the fact-checker at 0.1.
TokenThe unit AI services count text in — roughly three-quarters of a word. Speed, cost and rate limits are all measured in tokens.
Tool (agent sense)A function + a description that lets an AI ask for it to be run.

Job Agent · complete guide · generated July 2026 · source at github.com/DavidZeff1/agent
